claim: 69% of Podencos in a research study showed strong Hunt behavior, 69% Orient, 69% Chase, and 69% Grab-bite — the pursuit and capture phases dominate the predatory motor sequence.
stance_target: Podencos are overwhelmingly oriented toward active prey pursuit rather than passive monitoring when stimulated by prey signals
scope_conditions: Podencos studied in research context; sample size not specified in this article
quoted_support: Hunt: 69%, Orient: 69%, Eye: 54%, Stalk: 23%, Chase: 69%, Grab-bite: 69%, Possess: 31%, Kill-bite: 46%, Dissect: 23%, Consume: 8%

claim: Podencos score very low on Consume (8%) meaning their predatory motivation is driven by the chase itself rather than food reward — scent luring is less effective for recalling them mid-chase than it would be for food-motivated breeds.
stance_target: Behavioral prediction: food lures are less effective for recalling a Podenco in full prey-drive state compared to food-motivated breeds

claim: Podencos hunt using a combination of sight, scent, and hearing — they are not purely scent-led like a Beagle, nor purely sight-led like a Greyhound, making their lost-dog movement pattern a blend of scent-following and visual tracking.
stance_target: Podencos occupy a behavioral middle ground between scent hounds and sighthounds, requiring combined search strategies when lost
quoted_support: Podencos hunt using sight, scent and hearing.

claim: Tracey McLennan's research on predatory motor sequences in hunting dogs is published in Applied Animal Behaviour Science — the data cited here is peer-reviewed empirical research, not breed-enthusiast opinion.
stance_target: Podenco prey drive data has peer-reviewed empirical backing
quoted_support: I am a published researcher in my field with both my original research and my review of the literature looking at prey drive in dogs being published in the respected journal Applied Animal Behaviour Science.
